title: Understanding the Problem
text: To find the velocity of the fireworks shell when it leaves the mortar, we need to consider the energy transformations involved. The shell is initially at rest and gains kinetic energy as it is fired upwards.

title: Using Energy Conservation
text: The principle of conservation of energy states that the total mechanical energy (kinetic + potential) of the shell remains constant if we neglect air resistance.

title: Potential Energy at Maximum Height
text: At the maximum height of $$110.0$$ $$m$$, all the kinetic energy has been converted into potential energy. Use the formula for gravitational potential energy: $$PE=m g h$$, where $$m$$ is mass, g is acceleration due to gravity $$(9.8$$ m/s²), and $$h$$ is height.

title: Calculate Potential Energy
text: Calculate the potential energy of the shell at its maximum height using the formula $$PE=m g h$$.

title: Kinetic Energy at Launch
text: The kinetic energy at launch is equal to the potential energy at the maximum height. Use the formula $$KE=0.5m v^2$$ to find the initial velocity.

title: Calculate Initial Velocity
text: Set the kinetic energy equal to the potential energy and solve for v: $$0.5m v^2=PE$$.
